stories.headline: Avalanche Defeat Kings 2-1 in Game 1 of Stanley Cup Playoff First Round
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche opened their 2026 Stanley Cup Playoff first-round series against the Los Angeles Kings with a 2-1 victory Sunday at Ball Arena in Denver. Artturi Lehkonen scored the first goal of the game in the second period, helping the Presidents' Trophy-winning Avalanche take an early series lead.

Captain Gabriel Landeskog returned to playoff action for the first time in four years, skating on the team's third line after participating in Saturday's morning skate. The 33-year-old winger posted 14 goals and 35 points across 60 regular-season games before getting a day off for Thursday's season finale.

The Avalanche concluded the regular season by setting a franchise record for total points, winning the Presidents' Trophy for the best record in the NHL. Colorado also secured the William M. Jennings Trophy, awarded to the goaltenders whose team allowed the fewest goals, with Alexandar Georgiev and Scott Wedgewood sharing the honor — Wedgewood posted a 22-save shutout in the regular-season finale against Seattle.

Reports also emerged Sunday that the Avalanche are finalizing a deal to bring back 2021-22 Stanley Cup champion Nazem Kadri, which would be a significant addition to a roster already considered among the NHL's best heading into the postseason.

stories.headline: Rockies Rally Past Dodgers 9-6 on Sunday, Completing Weekend Split at Coors Field
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies defeated the Los Angeles Dodgers 9-6 on Sunday at Coors Field, with Mickey Moniak's two-run homer in the seventh inning sparking a comeback and the team collecting 15 hits in front of a crowd of 42,627. The win came one day after Troy Johnston's go-ahead two-run double in the sixth inning lifted the Rockies to a 4-3 Saturday victory over Los Angeles.

The Dodgers had beaten Colorado 7-1 on a snow-cleared Coors Field earlier in the series, with Max Muncy hitting two home runs. The Rockies' back-to-back wins against the reigning World Series champions provided a rare bright spot for a franchise rebuilding toward competitiveness.

Sunday's game also marked the 30th anniversary of the Colorado Rapids' first home game, celebrated amid a festive atmosphere in the stadium's surrounding neighborhood. The crowd was described as one of the louder early-season gatherings at Coors Field this year.

stories.headline: Nikola Jokic Named MVP Finalist for Sixth Straight Year; Two Other Nuggets Also Recognized
stories.summary:
The NBA announced Sunday that Denver Nuggets center Nikola Jokic is an MVP finalist for the sixth consecutive season, joining Oklahoma City's Shai Gilgeous-Alexander and San Antonio's Victor Wembanyama as the three finalists. Gilgeous-Alexander, who won the award in 2025 with Jokic as runner-up, is considered a heavy favorite to repeat.

Jokic, 31, averaged 27.7 points, 12.9 rebounds and 10.7 assists this season, becoming only the second player in NBA history to average a triple-double for an entire season in back-to-back years, joining Oscar Robertson and Russell Westbrook. A top-two finish would make him one of just three players — alongside Bill Russell and Larry Bird — to finish in the top two of MVP voting for six straight seasons. He has won the award three times.

Two other Nuggets also received award recognition. Guard Tim Hardaway Jr. is a Sixth Man of the Year finalist alongside Miami's Jaime Jaquez and San Antonio's Keldon Johnson. Guard Jamal Murray is a Clutch Player of the Year finalist alongside Gilgeous-Alexander and Minnesota's Anthony Edwards.

Jokic narrowly qualified for award ballots by playing his 65th game on the final night of the regular season, having missed four weeks in January with a hyperextended left knee and bone bruise. Award winners are typically announced during the second round of the playoffs.

stories.headline: Lakewood Voters' Rejection of Zoning Overhaul Highlights Statewide Housing Tensions
stories.summary:
Lakewood voters resoundingly rejected the City Council's sweeping zoning code overhaul in an April 7 special election, dealing a significant blow to months of work by elected officials who sought to permit denser housing — including triplexes and quadplexes — throughout Colorado's fifth-largest city. Mayor Wendi Strom described the initial results as "a kick in the gut" and said the city may need to take a different approach going forward.

The Lakewood Citizens Alliance, which led the referendum effort, argued the city attempted too many changes at once without adequately weighing residents' concerns about neighborhood character. The group collected enough signatures to place a repeal initiative on the ballot after the council passed the changes in December.

The outcome reflects a broader statewide pattern in which zoning reform efforts aimed at increasing housing affordability have faced voter backlash in communities including Fort Collins, Steamboat Springs and Littleton. Colorado had an estimated shortfall of 106,000 homes and apartments as of 2023 and needed to build at least 34,100 units per year to keep pace with population growth.

State Rep. Rebekah Stewart, a former Lakewood councilwoman who worked on earlier iterations of the changes, said the ordinances provided meaningful tools to address the housing shortage. Mayor Strom said the complexity of the zoning code made it difficult for average voters to evaluate and that future efforts would need to better engage residents throughout the process.

stories.headline: Aurora City Council to Consider Resolution of 'Mourning' for Officer-Involved Shooting Victims
stories.summary:
Aurora City Council members will discuss a resolution Monday that, if passed at a future regular meeting, would formally state that the council "stands in mourning" with families who have lost loved ones to officer-involved shootings by the Aurora Police Department. The measure was advanced out of the Public Safety, Courts and Civil Service Committee last week with little discussion.

The resolution was co-created by Councilmember Rob Andrews and the City Attorney's Office following nearly two years of protesters attending every City Council meeting to criticize APD and raise concerns about specific shootings. Notably, supporters of Kilyn Lewis — shot by SWAT Officer Michael Dieck on May 23, 2024, while unarmed during an attempted arrest — have repeatedly called for charges and the officer's firing. Dieck's use of force was deemed justified by multiple investigations.

Andrews emphasized the resolution is not directed at a more recent incident in which an officer shot and killed 23-year-old Amare James Garlington on April 9 after Garlington allegedly stabbed both the officer and a police dog. The officer and K-9 are expected to recover. The resolution explicitly notes that the council cannot comment publicly on ongoing legal cases.

Also on Monday's agenda is a return vote on a memorandum of understanding governing how Aurora police officers respond to the GEO Immigration and Customs Enforcement facility, which the council tabled at its previous meeting.

stories.headline: Denver City Council to Review $36 Million in Infrastructure, Airport Spending Monday
stories.summary:
The Denver City Council's Monday meeting includes several large spending items despite no scheduled public hearings. Chief among them is a $6.5 million contract for asphalt replacement covering 95 lane miles of local, collector and arterial streets across the city, including traffic control, signage and pavement markings.

Denver International Airport figures prominently in the agenda, with a pair of five-year contracts totaling $20 million for on-call landside facilities maintenance and repair services being awarded to Blue Sky Builders LLC and WCG Construction. DIA is also seeking a contract amendment with aviation consulting firm Ricondo & Associates adding two years and $750,000 — bringing the total to $2.25 million — for planning related to deicing infrastructure, taxiway configuration and a future runway.

The South Platte River Committee will also review a $9.7 million grant agreement with the Colorado Department of Transportation for improvements to the Alameda Avenue Underpass between Kalamath Street and Cherokee Street. The grant comes from CDOT and would fund work on the underpass corridor.

The council will also receive a proclamation honoring April 22 as Earth Day 56, sponsored by Councilmember Paul Kashmann. Meetings continue to be held in the Parr-Widener Community Room while renovations to the council chambers are ongoing; seating is limited.

stories.headline: Denver Neighborhoods Brace for Transformation as Broncos' Burnham Yard Stadium Plan Advances
stories.summary:
Community members and city officials in the La Alma-Lincoln Park neighborhood and surrounding areas are weighing the potential impacts of the Denver Broncos' plan to build a new stadium and 150-acre mixed-use development at the former Burnham Yard railyard site just southwest of downtown. The Broncos' project, which would open in 2031, represents one of the largest redevelopment efforts in Denver's recent history.

Two parallel processes are now underway to shape the project. The Burnham Yard Small Area Plan — a policy document developed between the city and the community — published a vision statement in late February calling for a "connected, inclusive" development "rooted in history, culture, and stewardship." It is targeting completion by the end of 2026. A separate Community Benefits Agreement, a legally binding document between the Broncos and community groups, is being developed with input from neighborhood associations.

District 3 City Council member Jamie Torres, whose district includes the area, said she won't celebrate until community concerns about displacement and equitable investment are addressed through those planning processes. The La Alma-Lincoln Park neighborhood has deep historical roots, including a Chicano mural from 1978 and Denver's oldest restaurant, The Buckhorn Exchange, established in 1893.

Burnham Yard was decommissioned in 2016 after transitioning from steam to diesel and electric trains over its century-long operational history. The railyard had long been an economic anchor for surrounding neighborhoods, and residents and businesses in the area are cautiously monitoring whether the Broncos-led development will bring promised benefits or accelerate gentrification.

stories.headline: Light Rail Service to Union Station Disrupted Sunday After Car Drives Onto Tracks
stories.summary:
Multiple RTD light rail lines were shut down Sunday afternoon after a car drove onto the tracks near Denver's Union Station, blocking A, B, G and N line service, according to the Regional Transportation District. The vehicle was first reported on the tracks at approximately 2 p.m., and a tow truck arrived on scene by 4:15 p.m. to remove it. Tracks remained closed as of 4:30 p.m.

RTD service alerts cited a trespasser on the tracks as the cause of the disruption. Bus shuttle service replaced the affected rail lines throughout the afternoon, running between stations impacted by the closure. The incident caused service interruptions on one of the system's busiest corridors, which connects Denver International Airport and several suburban communities to downtown.

RTD spokesperson Pauline Haberman confirmed the timeline and response but said information about why the car was left on the tracks was not immediately available Sunday. The incident is under investigation.

stories.headline: Colorado River Plans Draw Criticism as Federal Officials Take Emergency Action to Protect Lake Powell
stories.summary:
Water managers and experts across the Colorado River basin are raising alarms about five federal proposals to govern the drying river system, warning that none of the plans adequately account for the increasing frequency of critically dry years driven by climate change. The river provides water for approximately 40 million people in the American Southwest, including Colorado's Western Slope communities.

The U.S. Bureau of Reclamation took emergency action Friday to release hundreds of millions of gallons from Flaming Gorge Reservoir in Wyoming and Utah to prop up Lake Powell after updated projections showed spring flows into the reservoir could be less than a third of average. Without the emergency releases, Lake Powell risked dropping below "minimum power pool" — the level needed to operate hydropower turbines — and forcing water through smaller bypass tubes that are unsafe for extended use.

"In every one of those alternatives, under what they call critically dry hydrology, the system is failing," said Andy Mueller, general manager of the Colorado River District, a Glenwood Springs-based agency that advocates for Western Slope water users. He noted that critically dry conditions have been the norm for 25 years and should be expected to continue.

Negotiators from the seven states sharing the river have failed for more than two years to agree on a new long-term operating plan, making it increasingly likely that the federal government will impose its own framework. Experts warn that the current federal proposals would allow reservoir levels to repeatedly crash in dry years, triggering recurring emergency cuts to downstream water supplies and creating widespread uncertainty for farms and cities alike.

stories.headline: Two Injured Backcountry Skiers Rescued from Colorado Mountains Over Weekend
stories.summary:
Two backcountry skiers were rescued from separate incidents in Colorado's mountains over the weekend, with one airlifted by helicopter and both aided by Mountain Rescue Aspen crews, according to the Pitkin County Sheriff's Office. The first rescue occurred Saturday near Mace Peak, where a member of a four-person ski group sent a medical SOS at approximately 9:45 a.m. Mountain Rescue Aspen members who happened to be skiing in the area reached the injured person quickly and transported them via emergency sled to a Flight for Life helicopter landing zone.

The second rescue took place Sunday in the Lindley Hut area south of Ashcroft near Star Peak, after a skier sustained a knee injury and sent a medical SOS at 11:46 a.m. Three Mountain Rescue Aspen members evacuated the skier on an emergency sled, with 16 total search and rescue personnel assisting in coordination, communication and equipment support. All crews were out of the field by 3 p.m.

The Pitkin County Sheriff's Office reminded backcountry skiers to always carry a satellite communication device and exercise caution during the spring season, noting that hazards exist close to the surface of the snowpack. Both rescues highlight the ongoing risks of late-season backcountry travel in Colorado's high country.

stories.headline: Telluride's Income-Linked Rent Rules Leave Dozens of Units Empty, Displace Long-Term Residents
stories.summary:
New regulations in Telluride that tie rent to tenants' income have left the mountain town with nearly 50 empty units in early April across five town-owned housing complexes, raising concerns that the rules are undermining the affordable housing they were designed to provide. Long-term residents say the requirements are too rigid and fail to account for the seasonal nature of the region's economy.

Karla Sanchez, who spent five years raising four children in the Shandoka complex, saw her monthly rent jump from $1,800 to $3,600 after the town began counting the income of her 18-year-old twins who work locally and her college-attending son who comes home during breaks. She has since moved to Montrose and now commutes daily to get her daughters to school, leaving them waiting until nearly 11 p.m. each night before returning home.

Local attorney David Zemke, who receives approximately one call per week from tenants seeking help navigating the new rules, said many residents fell behind on required work hours and rent during a December ski patrol strike and resort shutdown — and now face potential eviction as they struggle to come back into compliance. "In a community that knows the seasonality of their economy that relies on seasonal workers, you would expect a little more empathy," Zemke said.

While Telluride is not part of the Denver metro area, the situation is drawing attention across Colorado as a cautionary tale about the unintended consequences of income-based housing policies in resort communities. The town is facing pressure to revisit the regulations amid mounting vacancies and resident departures.

stories.headline: Denver Heads Into Warm Week With Critical Fire Weather Expected Wednesday
stories.summary:
Denver and the broader Front Range are entering a warm and dry stretch following the weekend's cooler conditions, with temperatures forecast to climb into the 80s on the plains by Monday and Tuesday and skies remaining mostly clear. The mountain valleys are expected to reach the 60s, and light winds should keep fire weather concerns from becoming serious in the early part of the week.

Wednesday is flagged as the most dangerous day in the forecast, with gusty southwest winds, humidity dropping below 10 percent and temperatures again in the 80s combining to create critical fire weather conditions across much of the region. Denver7 meteorologists described it as "hot, windy, and very dry" — a rough combination that could elevate wildfire risk significantly for one day before conditions shift.

A more unsettled pattern is expected to develop late in the week, with a series of Pacific storm systems bringing increasing chances of rain and possibly mountain snow starting Thursday and extending into the following weekend. Forecasters note some uncertainty about precipitation totals but said there is a reasonable chance of meaningful moisture arriving before the week ends, along with cooler temperatures.

weather.notable: About 17 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: We had a lot of snow where I grew up. My dad would wake me up and say, 'Shovel the driveway.' I had a snow day and still had to work.

topStory.headline: U.S. Navy Seizes Iranian Cargo Ship Amid Naval Blockade, Threatening Fragile Ceasefire
topStory.summary:
The United States Navy fired on and seized an Iranian-flagged cargo ship named the Touska on Sunday after the vessel attempted to pass through the U.S. naval blockade near the Strait of Hormuz. President Donald Trump announced the interception on his Truth Social platform, writing that the nearly 900-foot ship ignored warnings to stop before a Navy vessel "blew a hole in the engine room" and took full custody of it. U.S. Central Command released footage of the interception, which showed a gun firing in the direction of the cargo ship.

Iran's top military headquarters, Khatam al-Anbiya, called the boarding an act of armed piracy and a violation of the existing ceasefire between the two countries, and vowed that Iranian armed forces would "soon respond" with retaliation. Iran's Deputy Foreign Minister Saeed Khatibzadeh said his country would not participate in a new round of face-to-face talks with the United States, citing what he described as Washington's "maximalist position." Iranian state media reported that officials would not attend any further negotiations while the U.S. blockade remained in place, and Iran's state news agency IRNA called reports of a second round of talks "not true."

The seizure is the first such interception since the United States imposed a naval blockade on Iranian ports last week, following an initial round of U.S.-Iran negotiations that ended without an agreement. The conflict between the two countries began with U.S. and Israeli strikes across Iran on February 28, followed by five weeks of attacks across the Middle East before a two-week truce was declared. Key unresolved issues include Iran's nuclear program and control of the Strait of Hormuz, a critical oil transport corridor.

Despite Iran's stated refusal to attend, the White House confirmed that a U.S. delegation led by Vice President JD Vance, and including advisers Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner, was set to arrive in Islamabad, Pakistan — the designated mediating country — on Monday. The ceasefire between the U.S. and Iran is scheduled to expire Wednesday, and Iran's military threat of retaliation following the Touska seizure places the durability of that truce in serious doubt. Trump said Friday that the naval blockade would remain in effect until a deal is reached, adding that the U.S. intended to recover approximately 970 pounds of enriched uranium believed to be buried under Iranian nuclear sites damaged in last year's strikes.
